When that gravity signal is removed, the inner ear has to recalibrate, muscles and bones are used differently, and fluids shift upward toward the head," Dr Aintablian says. "That is why we can see changes in balance, strength and, in some cases, vision." You'll have seen the astronauts using specialist exercise equipment during their journey, and that's to contend with the fact that their bodies are not being engaged by the same forces they are used to on Earth.

text: I'm glad that Artemis 2 launched successfully but there's nothing record-breaking about its mission. It is basically a simplified version of Apollo 8 but 58 years delayed. Neither mission planned a Moon landing but Apollo 8 orbited. Artemis 2's trajectory is a "free return." A rocket burn in Earth orbit injected Artemis 2 into a trans-lunar trajectory that "sling-shots" around the Moon back to Earth without another rocket burn. The fact that the crew is going further from Earth than humans have gone before is sold as an achievement but is just necessary to follow the simple "free return" trajectory. In later missions, Artemis will have to achieve lunar orbit to rendezvous with a lunar lander. So Artemis 2, while safer, is a less complete test than its predecessor, Apollo 8. Despite finally achieving crewed launch, Artemis 2 again demonstrates that today NASA can even replicate the tech achievement of its 1960s Apollo program. NASA's chronically underfunded human space flight has been, for decades, a bloated bureaucracy pushing globslist ideology not US space tech. It's time to turn US human space flight program over to the Space Force.
label: Critical/Skeptical

text: The crew of the Artemis 2 mission consists of four astronauts, three from NASA and one from the Canadian Space Agency (CSA). Here are the names and functions of each: Reid Wiseman (NASA): Mission Commander. Victor Glover (NASA): Pilot (will be the first black person to travel to the lunar neighborhood). Christina Koch (NASA): Mission Specialist (will be the first woman to fly on a lunar mission). Jeremy Hansen (CSA): Mission Specialist (will be the first Canadian and the first non-American to go to the Moon). Details of the Mission Artemis 2 is a historic landmark, being the first manned mission of the Artemis program. The goal is to perform an approximately 10-day flight around the Moon, testing all systems of the Orion capsule and the SLS rocket before the return of humans to the lunar surface on the next mission, Artemis 3. Interestingly, today (April 1, 2026) marks the official beginning of this journey, with the launch having taken place according to NASA's schedule.

text: NASA ArtemisII I completely agree that the Apollo Moon landings were not real. ( And question the "newest adventure ") However, those of us who question the official narrative must be careful not to undermine our position by sharing fake or fabricated "proofs." Spreading misinformation only hurts the credibility of legitimate skepticism. For example, claims about WikiLeaks publishing "staged cut scenes" from the Moon landing are false. WikiLeaks has released some unrelated NASA documents in the past — such as old emails discussing the reuse (erasure) of original Apollo videotapes in the 1970s — but nothing resembling leaked "behind-the-scenes" footage of a staged landing. Let's stick to verifiable evidence and avoid anything that can be easily debunked. Truth-seeking requires intellectual honesty. If we want people to take the inconsistencies in the Apollo story seriously, we need to hold ourselves to a higher standard than those promoting the official version.

This cost can be reduce for a single mission by having space stations above earth and the moon using what I say the best reusable space craft the shuttle for earth to space station and space station to the moon base!!! Yes there were two accidents with the space shuttle that cost a number of astronaut’s lives!!!! Since the start of the space race, roughly 20-30 astronauts and cosmonauts have died in spaceflight-related accidents, though only three have died directly in space (beyond the Kármán line). Major fatal incidents include the 1967 Apollo 1 fire (3 deaths), the 1971 Soyuz 11 mission (3 deaths), and the Space Shuttle Challenger (1986) and Columbia (2003) disasters (7 deaths each)!!! When you consider aircraft losses, since the first human spaceflight in 1961, over 19 people have died in space-related incidents, while tens of thousands have died in plane crashes on Earth. Aviation accidents since 1970 alone have resulted in over 83,000 fatalities, far exceeding the 19 astronaut/cosmonaut deaths during that same period!!! text: Its important to differentiate between the mission of Apollo 8 and Artemis 2. Apollo 8 fired engines to slow craft and drop into moon orbit and got as low as 70 miles from moon surface during its 10 orbits. Artemis 2 used the moon's gravity as a slingshot to return it to earth without any intervention by crew or NASA. No engines needed. Artemis 2 looped around the moon at a distance of about 4000 miles (never going into Orbit) compared to Apollo 8 orbiting 10 times at around 70 miles from the surface.
